title: RA.996 Ash Lauryn

translation.content: As part of our countdown to RA.1000, a milestone in the 19-year history of Resident Advisor's weekly mix series, we're switching up the usual format. The next few editions will pair two acts who compliment each other's strengths, offering a fresh take on a particular community, scene or style. Following on from two Nyege Nyege all-stars, this week we're zeroing in on two names who fly the flag high for old-school, US house—a foundational pillar of the electronic underground. The first half comes from Ron Trent, which you can read more about here. On the B-side is Ash Lauryn. Hailing from Detroit, she's a modern-day house savant. Her comprehensive understanding of the genre's history—knowledge gained from the Detroit greats she grew up around—and her own inimitable blend of old-world soul meets new-school grooves make her a force to be reckoned with. A former RA contributor, Lauryn keeps it real, whether in the booth or beyond. A role model to a new generation of DJs, she can just as easily be found teaching workshops at Underground Music Academy in Detroit or sharing tricks of the trade in the green rooms of the best clubs in the world. An unwavering champion of Black dance music, the Atlanta resident makes it a point to play as much Black American music as possible—as she told us in her 2019 RA Podcast. Not much has changed since then. Her contribution to RA.996 spans favourites like Larry Heard, Mood II Swing, Byron The Aquarius, Moodymann, Ron Trent and plenty of Detroit heavy-hitters. Listened to together, RA.996 is a powerful snapshot of house music's timeless elegance and, most importantly, understated yet innately euphoric joy. -Nyshka Chandran You've been running Underground & Black during Movement since 2017. As the number of parties happening that weekend grows every year, how has that impacted the way you approach U&B? To be honest, the party has built such a great reputation over the years that I don't worry too much about the other events affecting us. My approach hasn't changed much, outside of the addition of the techno / electro patio stage at the direction of my sister AMX in 2023, which, without question, elevated the party to new heights. It's also important to have a solid DJ lineup, but the overall authentic vibe of U&B Detroit sets it apart from the rest, and I'm grateful for that. Hosting the party at Spot Lite has been amazing, and it's a place most partygoers like to find themselves during the Movement Weekend festivities, which helps, too. You've been touring for several years now. How do you adapt your sound when playing big rooms and festival stages compared to intimate venues? For big rooms or festival sets, I like to play more upbeat and fun; those sets are also typically planned out a little more than an extended set in an intimate venue. Festival sets are usually no longer than 90 minutes, so you have to effectively spread your message in a short time—it helps to keep some bangers on deck. In regard to intimate venues, I like to get a little more experimental with my sound, and not necessarily play all house-oriented tunes. Smaller venues, I like to start slow and smooth and see where the night takes us. What is your dream for Detroit's electronic music industry? Is there anything you'd like to see happen for the city's scene to keep growing and sustaining itself? My dream is for the city's scene to keep thriving and for the youth to continue involving themselves. The longevity of Detroit techno and its culture is truly dependent on its preservation, and we need folks who are passionate about keeping the sound and scene alive. Initiatives like the Underground Music Academy are doing great work and community outreach through workshops, mentorship, and more. Correction, July 9th: The RA Podcast started 18 years ago, in 2006, not 24.
translation.blurb: Part two of RA.996 comes from a modern-day house luminary.
